test_description: For each masked nside=256 temperature map, remove the monopole and dipole on the unmasked sky, estimate the low-l harmonic coefficients for l=2 and l=3 using the same masked-sky least-squares spherical-harmonic fit for data and simulations, then find for each multipole the unit axis n_l that maximizes the angular-momentum dispersion sum_m m^2 |a_lm(n_l)|^2 after rotating that axis to the z-direction. The single statistic is S = |n_2 · n_3|, the absolute cosine of the angle between the quadrupole and octopole preferred axes; values near 1 indicate unusually strong alignment.
test_hypothesis: The observed CMB quadrupole and octopole preferred axes are more strongly aligned than expected from isotropic simulations, i.e. Planck has unusually large S = |n2 · n3|.
justificaton: The statistic increases toward 1 for stronger quadrupole-octopole alignment, and the code explicitly treats the upper tail as the tail of interest using empirical_p_upper_alignment and flags high-alignment outliers. The anomaly being tested is excessive alignment, not unusually weak alignment.

custom_summary.test_description: Quadrupole-Octopole Axis Alignment: remove the best-fit monopole and dipole on finite unmasked pixels, estimate l=2 and l=3 coefficients with the same equal-weight masked-sky least-squares spherical-harmonic fit, and return S = |n2 dot n3| where each n_l maximizes angular-momentum dispersion. Values near 1 indicate strong alignment.
custom_summary.tail_of_interest: upper tail: S near 1 means stronger quadrupole-octopole axis alignment
custom_summary.ideal_independent_axes_reference: For two independent isotropic unoriented axes, |dot| is uniform on [0,1]; empirical ranks below use the masked simulations.

plot_spec.title: Quadrupole-octopole axis alignment
plot_spec.caption: Distribution of the absolute dot product between quadrupole and octopole maximum-angular-momentum axes. Larger values indicate stronger alignment.

custom_summary.rank_convention: Descending rank 1 is largest S, i.e. strongest alignment; tie_high includes simulations tied with Planck.
custom_summary.interpretation: Planck is not in the high-alignment tail at the 5% empirical level for this statistic.

test_summary.Description: Planck and 1000 isotropic simulations were analysed with the same masked-sky least-squares low-l harmonic fit after monopole/dipole removal. The statistic was S = |n2 · n3|, where n2 and n3 maximize angular-momentum dispersion for the quadrupole and octopole; larger S means stronger alignment.
test_summary.Results: Planck gave S = 0.290, corresponding to an angle of 73.1 deg. Simulations had mean S = 0.530, median 0.540, and standard deviation 0.294. The upper-tail p-value for stronger alignment was 0.744, with Planck at the 25.6th percentile rather than in the high-alignment tail.
test_summary.Interpretation: The observed axes are less aligned than a typical simulation and are far from the anomalous upper tail. This test therefore gives no support for unusually strong Planck quadrupole-octopole alignment under this implementation.
test_summary.Comparison to literature: The supplied search results did not identify a closely matching masked-sky least-squares implementation. However, quadrupole-octopole alignment itself is a well-known CMB anomaly statistic, discussed for example by de Oliveira-Costa et al. 2004, Schwarz et al. 2004, Copi et al. 2010, and Planck isotropy analyses. Unlike many reported full-sky low-l alignment claims, this result finds no high-alignment anomaly.
